docs.title: Finding Valid PartNumbers
docs.text: PartNumber values are slugs from the device-type library under pkg/devicetypes/. Browse the YAML files there for available rack types, device types, module types, and cable types.

docs.title: Visual Output
docs.text: The show command displays inventory data with sorting, filtering, and visual hierarchy options.

docs.title: Show Inventory
docs.text:
# Show all devices
cani alpha show device

# Show all racks
cani alpha show rack

# Show all locations
cani alpha show location

# Show cables
cani alpha show cables

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text:
# Show devices filtered by role
cani alpha show device --role Compute

# Show devices filtered by type
cani alpha show device --type blade

docs.title: Visual Hierarchy
docs.text:
The show command can render a tree view of the inventory:

# Show a tree view of all inventory
cani alpha show device --tree

docs.title: Exporting Data
docs.text: After adding, removing, updating, or classifying hardware in the local inventory, the export command pushes the changes to an external provider.

docs.title: Export To A Provider
docs.text:
# Export to a Nautobot instance
cani alpha export nautobot --url http://localhost:8081 --token $NAUTOBOT_TOKEN

# Export to CSM SLS
cani alpha export csm \
  --csm-keycloak-username username \
  --csm-keycloak-password password \
  --csm-api-host api-gw-service-nmn.local

docs.title: Dry Run
docs.text:
Preview the changes without making any API calls:

# See what would be pushed without actually pushing
cani alpha export nautobot --dry-run

docs.title: Merge Mode
docs.text:
By default, conflicts are skipped. Use --merge to combine with existing data:

# Merge with existing devices instead of skipping conflicts
cani alpha export nautobot --merge

docs.title: Importing Data
docs.text: The import command pulls inventory data from an external provider into the local datastore. Each provider has its own flags for authentication and connection details.

docs.title: Import From A Provider
docs.text:
# Import from a Nautobot instance
cani alpha import nautobot --url http://localhost:8081 --token $NAUTOBOT_TOKEN

# Import from a Redfish ServiceRoot JSON file
cani alpha import redfish --root ./redfish-roots.json

# Import from an Ochami export
cani alpha import ochami --source ./ochami-export.json

# Import from a CSV or YAML file using the example provider
cani alpha import example --source ./inventory.csv

docs.title: CSM Provider
docs.text:
The CSM provider connects to SLS. Credentials and the API host are required.

# Import from CSM over the CMN
cani alpha import csm \
  --csm-keycloak-username username \
  --csm-keycloak-password password \
  --csm-api-host cmn.example.com

# Import from CSM on an NCN
cani alpha import csm \
  --csm-keycloak-username username \
  --csm-keycloak-password password \
  --csm-api-host api-gw-service-nmn.local

docs.title: What Happens During Import
docs.text:
Import runs the provider's ETL pipeline:

  1. Extract — Pull raw data from the external source
  2. Transform — Convert to the portable inventory format
  3. Load — Save the result to the local datastore

Once imported, the inventory can be manipulated with add, remove, update, classify, and show commands before exporting back.

docs.title: ETL Pipeline
docs.text: Each provider follows an Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) pattern when importing data. This pipeline converts provider-specific data into the portable inventory format.

docs.title: Extract
docs.text: The provider connects to the external source and pulls raw data. This might be a REST API call (Nautobot, CSM), a JSON file (Redfish, Ochami), or a flat file (Example).

docs.title: Transform
docs.text: Raw data is converted to the inventory data model. Device types are matched against the device-types library, and parent-child relationships are established.

docs.title: Load
docs.text: The transformed inventory is saved to the local datastore. Subsequent commands (add, remove, update, classify, show) work against this local copy.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# 1. Import devices from Redfish
cani alpha import redfish --root ./redfish-roots.json

# 2. Classify unrecognized devices
cani alpha classify --auto

# 3. Add racks if they were not included in the import
cani alpha add rack hpe-48u-800mmx1200mm-g2-enterprise-shock-rack

# 4. Resolve orphaned devices (those without a parent rack)
cani alpha update orphans

# 5. Export to Nautobot
cani alpha export nautobot --url http://localhost:8081 --token $NAUTOBOT_TOKEN

docs.title: Injected Metadata
docs.text:
Providers may inject metadata into the inventory during import. For example, the CSM provider stores schema versioning:

"ExtraProperties": {
    "@cani.id": "ebb15e9a-f418-408c-8315-601483f3b279",
    "@cani.lastModified": "2023-07-03 18:24:29.12421 +0000 UTC",
    "@cani.slsSchemaVersion": "v1alpha1"
}

This metadata enables cani to track provenance and detect schema changes across imports.

docs.title: Resolving Orphans
docs.text: When devices are imported without a parent rack or location, they are considered "orphaned." The update orphans command resolves these by interactively assigning parents.

docs.title: Interactive Resolution
docs.text:
# Walk through all orphaned racks and devices
cani alpha update orphans

Each orphan is presented with candidate parents ranked by:

  • Name similarity
  • Hardware-type affinity
  • Provider metadata

docs.title: Dry Run
docs.text:
Preview the assignments without modifying the inventory:

# Save assignments to a plan file
cani alpha update orphans --dry-run

The plan file is saved to ~/.cani/resolve-plan.json and can be reviewed or edited before applying.

docs.title: Apply A Plan
docs.text:
Apply a previously saved plan file:

# Apply the plan without prompting
cani alpha update orphans --apply-plan ~/.cani/resolve-plan.json

docs.title: Typical Workflow
docs.text:
Orphan resolution is usually needed after importing from sources that don't include rack placement:

# 1. Import devices (some may not have rack info)
cani alpha import redfish --root ./redfish-roots.json

# 2. Add racks if needed
cani alpha add rack hpe-48u-800mmx1200mm-g2-enterprise-shock-rack

# 3. Resolve orphans
cani alpha update orphans

docs.title: Updating Hardware
docs.text: Some providers and certain types of hardware require additional metadata. The update command modifies existing inventory items.

docs.title: Update A Device
docs.text:
After importing or adding devices, they may need metadata like roles or aliases:

# Update a device's role and alias
cani alpha update device --role Compute --alias nid00001

# Update a device's NID
cani alpha update device --nid 1

docs.title: Update With Classification
docs.text:
Devices imported from Redfish or other sources may not have a device-type slug. Use classify first, then update:

# Import from Redfish
cani alpha import redfish --root ./redfish-roots.json

# Classify unrecognized devices
cani alpha classify --auto

# Update metadata
cani alpha update device --role Compute --alias nid00001 --nid 1

docs.title: Bulk Updates
docs.text:
The update set command applies changes to multiple items at once:

# Set the role for all devices matching a filter
cani alpha update set --role Compute

docs.title: Go Install
docs.text:
1. Install Go.
  2. Clone the cani repo: https://github.com/Cray-HPE/cani.git
  3. Run or build the binary
go run main.go # run the app directly using go
make bin # use the makefile to make the binary with support for the version subcommand
go build -o bin/cani # quickly build without support for the version subcommand

docs.title: RPM Install
docs.text:
Install the latest RPM.

rpm -ivh cani.rpm

docs.title: ObjectMeta
docs.text:
Every inventory type (device, rack, location, module, cable, FRU, interface) embeds the same ObjectMeta struct. This provides a uniform set of metadata fields across all hardware:

type ObjectMeta struct {
    Status           string
    Role             string
    Tags             []string
    Tenant           string
    CustomFields     map[string]any
    ExternalIDs      map[string]uuid.UUID
    ProviderMetadata map[string]any
}

Because ObjectMeta is embedded in Go, these fields appear directly on each type. In JSON they are serialized as flat fields:

{
  "id": "f7448392-...",
  "name": "nid000001",
  "status": "active",
  "role": "Compute",
  "tags": ["production", "gpu-node"],
  "tenant": "HPC-East",
  "providerMetadata": {
    "csm": { "xname": "x3000c0s3b0n0", "nid": 1 }
  }
}

docs.title: Inventory Metadata Catalog
docs.text:
The inventory carries a top-level metadata field that stores the global catalog of roles, statuses, and tags available for assignment:

{
  "schemaVersion": "v1alpha2",
  "metadata": {
    "roles": [
      { "name": "ComputeNode", "contentTypes": ["dcim.device"], "weight": 1000 }
    ],
    "statuses": [
      { "name": "Active", "color": "green" }
    ],
    "tags": [
      { "name": "gpu-node", "description": "Nodes with GPUs" }
    ]
  }
}

Add entries with the CLI:

cani alpha add metadata role ComputeNode --content-types dcim.device
cani alpha add metadata status Active --color green
cani alpha add metadata tag gpu-node --description "Nodes with GPUs"

docs.title: ProviderMetadata
docs.text:
Devices and racks carry a providerMetadata field: a map[string]any keyed by provider name. Each provider stores its own arbitrary data under its key, so multiple providers can coexist without colliding.

"providerMetadata": {
  "csm": {
    "xname": "x3000c0s3b0n0",
    "class": "Mountain",
    "role": "Compute",
    "nid": 1
  },
  "redfish": {
    "redfish_uuid": "abc-123",
    "bmc_fqdn": "bmc1.example.com"
  }
}

The top-level key ("csm", "redfish", etc.) identifies which provider wrote the data. The nested map can contain anything that provider needs.

docs.title: CSM
docs.text: Key Type Description xname string Location-based component name (e.g. x3000c0s3b0n0) class string Hardware class (Mountain, River, Hill) role string Node role (Compute, Management, Application) subRole string Node sub-role (Worker, Master, Storage) nid int Node ID number aliases []string Hostname aliases state string Hardware state from HSM hmnVlan int Hardware Management Network VLAN ID

docs.title: Redfish
docs.text: Key Type Description redfish_uuid string UUID reported by the Redfish service bmc_fqdn string BMC fully-qualified domain name

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Key Type Description Source string Import source (e.g. the CSV file path) PartNumber string Part number from the CSV record ConfigGroup string Configuration group from the CSV record

docs.title: HPCM
docs.text: The HPCM provider stores a free-form map under the "hpcm" key. Common fields include node_uuid, location, aliases, and other node attributes from the HPCM JSON export.

docs.title: Custom Providers
docs.text:
Any provider can write arbitrary keys under its own namespace. The only requirement is that the top-level key matches the provider name. This makes providerMetadata fully extensible — a custom provider can store whatever data it needs without a schema change.

"providerMetadata": {
  "my-provider": {
    "custom_key": "custom_value",
    "nested": { "anything": true }
  }
}

docs.title: ExternalIDs
docs.text:
The externalIDs field maps provider names to the remote UUID that provider uses for this item. This is used during export to update the correct remote record.

"externalIDs": {
  "nautobot": "9a8b7c6d-5e4f-3a2b-1c0d-000000000001"
}

docs.title: CustomFields
docs.text: The customFields field is a general-purpose map[string]any for user- or provider-defined data that does not belong under a specific provider namespace.

docs.title: Tags
docs.text: The tags field is a string slice for arbitrary labels (e.g. ["production", "gpu-node"]).

docs.title: Inventory Format Overview
docs.text: The inventory is a versioned datastore that holds every piece of hardware cani knows about. Each hardware category is stored as a separate map keyed by UUID.

docs.title: Top-Level Structure
docs.text:
In Go the inventory is the Inventory struct:

type Inventory struct {
    SchemaVersion string
    Provider      string

    Locations  map[uuid.UUID]*CaniLocationType
    Racks      map[uuid.UUID]*CaniRackType
    Devices    map[uuid.UUID]*CaniDeviceType
    Modules    map[uuid.UUID]*CaniModuleType
    Cables     map[uuid.UUID]*CaniCableType
    Frus       map[uuid.UUID]*CaniFruType
    Interfaces map[uuid.UUID]*CaniInterface

    Metadata   *InventoryMetadata // catalog of roles, statuses, tags
}

The current schema version is v1alpha2.

docs.title: Hardware Types
docs.text: Type Go Type Description Location CaniLocationType Physical site, building, floor, or room Rack CaniRackType Equipment rack with U-slot tracking Device CaniDeviceType Server, switch, PDU, chassis, or blade Module CaniModuleType Component installed in a device (GPU, NIC, PSU) Cable CaniCableType Physical cable between two endpoints FRU CaniFruType Field-replaceable unit (spare or replacement part) Interface CaniInterface Network or console port on a device or module

docs.title: Relationships
docs.text:
Items reference each other by UUID:

  • A Location has Children (child locations) and Racks.
  • A Rack has a Location FK and a Devices list.
  • A Device has Parent, Children, Rack, Location, and Frus references.
  • A Module has a ParentDevice FK.
  • A Cable has TerminationA and TerminationB endpoint UUIDs.
  • A FRU has a Device or Parent FK.

docs.title: Example: Device
docs.text:
{
  "f7448392-1e1c-45d0-9c59-be7dfc44c15c": {
    "id": "f7448392-1e1c-45d0-9c59-be7dfc44c15c",
    "name": "nid000001",
    "type": "Device",
    "manufacturer": "HPE",
    "model": "EX420 Compute Blade",
    "status": "active",
    "role": "Compute",
    "parent": "7e3de0fa-e3d6-421b-9d25-c0192d2a5966",
    "rack": "a1b2c3d4-0000-0000-0000-000000000001",
    "rackPosition": 3,
    "children": [
      "00050177-a309-4fde-bf85-70452b228e24",
      "004ecb7f-50bb-4975-9973-b6c617d6cc82"
    ],
    "providerMetadata": {
      "csm": {
        "xname": "x3000c0s3b0n0",
        "class": "Mountain",
        "role": "Compute",
        "nid": 1
      }
    },
    "externalIDs": {
      "nautobot": "9a8b7c6d-5e4f-3a2b-1c0d-000000000001"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Common Fields
docs.text:
All types embed ObjectMeta, providing a uniform set of metadata fields:

  • id — unique UUID identifier
  • name — human-readable name
  • status — lifecycle state (staged, active, planned, etc.)
  • role — functional role (e.g. Compute, Spine Switch)
  • tags — arbitrary string labels
  • tenant — tenant or ownership group
  • customFields — free-form key/value map
  • externalIDs — maps a provider name to that provider's remote UUID
  • providerMetadata — provider-specific data (see Metadata)

docs.title: UUID
docs.text: Every item in the inventory — whether a location, rack, device, module, cable, FRU, or interface — is assigned a unique UUID (v4) when it is created. This UUID is the primary identifier the system uses for all read and write operations.

docs.title: How UUIDs Are Used
docs.text:
• Map keys — Each hardware map in the Inventory is keyed by UUID, giving O(1) lookups.
  • Parent/child references — Relationships between items (e.g. a device's parent, a rack's devices list) are stored as UUIDs, not names.
  • Provider deduplication — When importing data, the system checks whether an incoming item already exists by looking up provider-specific keys (via the provider-key index) before assigning a new UUID. This prevents duplicates across repeated imports.
  • Export targeting — During export, externalIDs maps a provider name to the remote UUID so the system knows which remote record to update.

docs.title: Generation
docs.text: UUIDs are generated with uuid.New() (Google's UUID v4 implementation). Once assigned, a UUID never changes for the lifetime of that inventory item.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
{
  "f7448392-1e1c-45d0-9c59-be7dfc44c15c": {
    "id": "f7448392-1e1c-45d0-9c59-be7dfc44c15c",
    "name": "nid000001",
    "type": "Device",
    "status": "active"
  }
}

The key f7448392-1e1c-45d0-9c59-be7dfc44c15c and the id field always match. The key is used for map lookups; the id field makes the identifier accessible when the item is serialized on its own.

docs.title: CSM Provider
docs.text: The CSM provider imports from and exports to SLS (System Layout Service), CSM's hardware inventory backend.

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
# Import from CSM on an NCN
cani alpha import csm \
  --csm-keycloak-username username \
  --csm-keycloak-password password \
  --csm-api-host api-gw-service-nmn.local

# Import from CSM over the CMN
cani alpha import csm \
  --csm-keycloak-username username \
  --csm-keycloak-password password \
  --csm-api-host cmn.example.com

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
# Export to CSM
cani alpha export csm \
  --csm-keycloak-username username \
  --csm-keycloak-password password \
  --csm-api-host api-gw-service-nmn.local

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Provider-specific options in ~/.cani/cani.yml:

providers:
  csm:
    keycloak_username: ""       # Keycloak username
    keycloak_password: ""       # Keycloak password
    api_host: ""                # API gateway host
    ca_cert: ""                 # Path to CA certificate (optional)
    insecure: false             # Skip TLS verification (not recommended)

docs.title: Example Provider
docs.text: The Example provider is a reference implementation for bootstrapping new providers. It imports from CSV or YAML files and exports a visual hierarchy.

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
# Import from a CSV file
cani alpha import example --source ./inventory.csv

# Import from a YAML file
cani alpha import example --source ./inventory.yaml

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
# Export a visual hierarchy of the inventory
cani alpha export example

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
The Example provider is primarily intended as:

  • A reference for developing new providers
  • A way to bootstrap an inventory from flat files (CSV/YAML)
  • A debugging tool for viewing the inventory hierarchy

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Provider-specific options in ~/.cani/cani.yml:

providers:
  example:
    import:
      # Import-specific options

docs.title: HPCM Provider
docs.text: The HPCM provider imports hardware inventory from HPE Performance Cluster Manager node JSON exports and cm.config files. This enables migration of existing HPCM-managed clusters into cani's unified inventory.

docs.title: From A Node JSON File
docs.text:
# Import from an HPCM node JSON export
cani alpha import hpcm --node-json-file ./hpcm-nodes.json

docs.title: From A cm.config File
docs.text:
# Import from an HPCM cm.config file
cani alpha import hpcm --cm-config ./cm.config

docs.title: From Both Sources
docs.text:
When both flags are provided, nodes are merged and deduplicated by name:

cani alpha import hpcm --node-json-file ./hpcm-nodes.json --cm-config ./cm.config

docs.title: From Stdin
docs.text:
If --node-json-file is omitted, node JSON can be piped from stdin:

cat hpcm-nodes.json | cani alpha import hpcm

docs.title: Export
docs.text: Export is not yet implemented for the HPCM provider.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Devices imported from HPCM are classified based on their node type (compute, service, switch, storage, etc.). Some devices may need manual classification:

# Import from HPCM
cani alpha import hpcm --node-json-file ./hpcm-nodes.json

# Auto-classify the imported devices
cani alpha classify --auto

See Classifying Devices for more detail.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Provider-specific options in ~/.cani/cani.yml:

providers:
  hpcm:
    import:
      node_json_file: ""      # Path to HPCM node JSON file
      cm_config_file: ""      # Path to HPCM cm.config file

docs.title: Nautobot Provider
docs.text: The Nautobot provider imports from and exports to Nautobot DCIM instances (Netbox-compatible). It uses the Nautobot REST API to sync devices, racks, and device types.

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
# Import from a Nautobot instance
cani alpha import nautobot --url http://localhost:8081 --token $NAUTOBOT_TOKEN

docs.title: Export
docs.text:
# Export to a Nautobot instance
cani alpha export nautobot --url http://localhost:8081 --token $NAUTOBOT_TOKEN

# Preview changes without making API calls
cani alpha export nautobot --dry-run

# Merge with existing devices instead of skipping conflicts
cani alpha export nautobot --merge

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Provider-specific options in ~/.cani/cani.yml:

providers:
  nautobot:
    url: ""                     # Base URL of the Nautobot instance
    token: ""                   # API token for authentication
    default_location: ""        # Default location for devices
    default_role: ""            # Default role for devices
    default_status: ""          # Default status for devices
    import:
      # Import-specific options
    export:
      # Export-specific options

docs.title: Ochami Provider
docs.text: The Ochami provider imports hardware inventory from OpenCHAMI hardware database exports.

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
# Import from an Ochami JSON export file
cani alpha import ochami --source ./ochami-export.json

docs.title: Export
docs.text: Export is not yet implemented for the Ochami provider.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Provider-specific options in ~/.cani/cani.yml:

providers:
  ochami:
    import:
      # Import-specific options

docs.title: Redfish Provider
docs.text: The Redfish provider imports hardware inventory from Redfish ServiceRoot JSON data. This is commonly used for BMC/iLO discovery from any Redfish-compliant endpoint.

docs.title: From A File
docs.text:
# Import from a Redfish ServiceRoot JSON file
cani alpha import redfish --root ./redfish-roots.json

docs.title: From Stdin
docs.text:
# Pipe Redfish data from another tool
curl -s https://bmc01.example.com/redfish/v1 | cani alpha import redfish --root -

docs.title: Export
docs.text: Export is not yet implemented for the Redfish provider.

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
Devices imported from Redfish may not have a device-type slug assigned. Use the classify command to resolve them:

# Import from Redfish
cani alpha import redfish --root ./redfish-roots.json

# Auto-classify the imported devices
cani alpha classify --auto

See Classifying Devices for more detail.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Provider-specific options in ~/.cani/cani.yml:

providers:
  redfish:
    import:
      root: ""                  # Path to Redfish ServiceRoot JSON file

docs.title: Known Errors
docs.text: This page lists known errors organized by provider. Select a provider below to see its specific errors and solutions.

docs.title: By Provider
docs.text:
Provider Description CSM SLS network and xname issues 

### Convert The Strings To Integers In SLS Using `jq`

Get the affected xnames into an array for looping through.

```shell
# via cray
xnames=($(cray sls dumpstate list --format json  | jq -r '[.Hardware[] | select(.ExtraProperties.NID | type == "string") | .Xname] | join(" ")'))

# or via curl
xnames=($(curl https://api-gw-service-nmn.local/apis/sls/v1/dumpstate | jq -r '[.Hardware[] | select(.ExtraProperties.NID | type == "string") | .Xname] | join(" ")'))

Dump each hardware component into a file and edit their values back to integers.

cray sls dumpstate list --format json | jq '.Hardware[] | select(.ExtraProperties.NID | type == "string") | .ExtraProperties.NID |= tonumber' > fixed.json

Loop through each fixed entry and update it in SLS.

for xname in "${xnames[@]}"
do
  # via cray
  jq --arg xname "${xname}" '. | select(.Xname == $xname)' < fixed.json > "${xname}.json"
  cray sls hardware update "${xname}" --payload-file "${xname}.json"

  # or via curl
  curl -X 'PUT' https://api-gw-service-nmn.local/apis/sls/v1/hardware/"${xname}" -H "'Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}'" -H 'Accept: application/json' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d "$(jq -c -n --arg xname "${xname}" 'inputs | select(.Xname == $xname) | .' < fixed.json)"
done

docs.title: CSM Known Errors
docs.text: Known errors and solutions specific to the CSM provider.

docs.title: Required network HMN_MTN is missing or Required network NMN_MTN is missing
docs.text:
For the CSM provider, this means the existing SLS data is unstable as the Networks key is missing a required field.

To fix the issue, craft an appropriate JSON file and use it to create the network in SLS.

docs.title: Create An HMN_MTN or NMN_MTN Network JSON File
docs.text:
Here is an example hmn_mtn.json file that creates the network when patched into SLS.

Note: update the site-specific information as needed (IP, VLANs, etc.)

{
  "Name": "HMN_MTN",
  "FullName": "Mountain Compute Hardware Management Network",
  "IPRanges": [
    "10.104.0.0/17"
  ],
  "Type": "ethernet",
  "ExtraProperties": {
    "CIDR": "10.104.0.0/17",
    "MTU": 9000,
    "Subnets": [],
    "VlanRange": [
      2000,
      2999
    ]
  }
}

Here is the same, but for nmn_mtn.json.

Note: update the site-specific information as needed (IP, VLANs, etc.)

{
  "Name": "NMN_MTN",
  "FullName": "Mountain Compute Node Management Network",
  "IPRanges": [
    "10.100.0.0/17"
  ],
  "Type": "ethernet",
  "ExtraProperties": {
    "CIDR": "10.100.0.0/17",
    "MTU": 9000,
    "Subnets": [],
    "VlanRange": [
      3000,
      3999
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Create The Networks in SLS
docs.text:
Use the cray command to add the network(s):

# Backup SLS
cray sls dumpstate list --format json > sls_dump_"$(date +"%s")".json 

# Patch in the appropriate network using the JSON files created earlier
cray sls networks update ./hmn_mtn.json HMN_MTN 
cray sls networks update ./nmn_mtn.json NMN_MTN

docs.title: Hardware/<XNAME>/ExtraProperties/NID: expected integer, but got string
docs.text:
This appears when a NID number in the ExtraProperties.NID key is populated with a string instead of an integer.

Inspect each NID for any strings.

cray sls dumpstate list --format json | jq '.Hardware[] | select(.ExtraProperties.NID | type == "string") | .ExtraProperties'

The "2" and "3" in the output below cause the issue in this example.

{
  "Aliases": [
    "nid000002"
  ],
  "NID": "2",
  "Role": "Compute"
}
{
  "Aliases": [
    "nid000003"
  ],
  "NID": "3",
  "Role": "Compute"
}
